content:
How Does a Senior Front-End Developer Think?

Technical Decision-Making, Avoiding Overengineering, Building a Healthy Code Review Culture, and Managing Technical Debt

At the beginning of every front-end developer’s journey, most of the focus is placed on learning tools and technologies:

• How do I write JavaScript?

• How do I use React, Vue, or Angular?

• How do I consume APIs?

• How do I build reusable components?

• How do I manage application state?

• How do I create responsive layouts?

These questions are extremely important. They form the technical foundation that every front-end developer needs.

However, after several years of professional experience, developers usually discover that writing code is not the most difficult part of software development.

The truly difficult parts are often:

• Making the right technical decision for a specific project.

• Choosing the appropriate level of complexity.

• Balancing delivery speed with code quality.

• Managing disagreements inside the engineering team.

• Knowing when a system needs refactoring.

• Knowing when existing code should be left alone.

• Managing technical debt before it becomes a serious problem.

• Protecting the project from overengineering.

• Conducting code reviews that improve the team rather than slow it down.

The real difference between a junior and a senior front-end developer is not that the senior developer knows more libraries or can write more complicated code.

The real difference is that a senior developer can make reasonable decisions in imperfect conditions.

Real-world software development rarely happens inside an ideal project.

It happens inside projects that have:

• Tight deadlines.

• Limited budgets.

• Developers with different experience levels.

• Frequently changing requirements.

• Legacy code.

• Pressure from management or clients.

• Production incidents that must be fixed quickly.

• Real users who are affected by every mistake.

This article discusses four essential areas of senior front-end engineering:

• How to make technical decisions.

• How to avoid overengineering.

• How to conduct effective code reviews.

• How to manage technical debt.

Part One: How Does a Senior Front-End Developer Make Technical Decisions?

Technical decisions are not competitions for choosing the “best” technology

One of the most common mistakes in software development is treating technical decisions like competitions to identify the best technology available.

Teams often ask questions such as:

What is the best state management library?

What is the best front-end framework?

Should we use micro-frontends?

Should we build our own design system?

The problem is that these questions are usually incomplete.

There is no technology that is objectively best in every situation.

The better question is not:

What is the best technology?

The better question is:

Which technology is the most appropriate for this project, this team, this stage, and these constraints?

Redux may be an excellent choice for one project and an unnecessary burden for another.

Next.js may be a strong choice for a platform that heavily depends on search engine optimization and server rendering, but it may introduce unnecessary complexity into a private internal dashboard.

Micro-frontends may be reasonable for an organization with dozens of independent engineering teams, but they may be a disastrous decision for a team of three developers.

Building an internal design system may be a valuable long-term investment for a large platform, but it may be a waste of time for an MVP that must launch within four weeks.

A technical decision should not be judged by how modern or impressive the technology looks.

It should be judged by how effectively it solves the real problem.

Start with the problem, not the tool

A less experienced developer may begin with a technology:

I want to use Zustand.

I want to try GraphQL.

I want to build the project using micro-frontends.

I want to apply Clean Architecture everywhere.

A more experienced developer begins with the problem:

• What problem are we trying to solve?

• Who is affected by it?

• What is the business impact?

• Is this a current problem or a hypothetical future problem?

• Do we have evidence that the problem exists?

• Do we need to solve it now?

• What is the simplest solution that can handle it?

• What will the solution cost the team?

Before proposing any technology, define the problem clearly.

For example, instead of saying:

We need Redux.

Say:

The current user, permissions, shopping cart, and filters are being passed through several component levels, and managing them through props is becoming difficult.

Now the problem is clear, and the team can compare multiple possible solutions:

• React Context.

• Zustand.

• Redux Toolkit.

• Component restructuring.

• Moving server state into React Query.

• Moving filters into the URL.

• Keeping some data local to the feature.

The team may discover that the actual problem is not the lack of a state management library.

The real problem may be that different categories of state are being mixed together.

Types of technical decisions in front-end development

A senior front-end developer makes technical decisions at several levels.

1. Technology selection decisions

Examples include:

• React, Vue, or Angular?

• TypeScript or JavaScript?

• Next.js or a client-side React application?

• REST or GraphQL?

• Redux, Zustand, or Context?

• Tailwind CSS, CSS Modules, or CSS-in-JS?

• An existing UI library or an internal design system?

2. Architectural decisions

Examples include:

• How should the project be structured?

• Should files be organized by technical type or by feature?

• Where should business logic live?

• How should the API layer be designed?

• How should errors be handled?

• How should authentication be implemented?

• How should authorization and permissions be represented?

• What are the boundaries of each module?

3. Quality-related decisions

Examples include:

• Which parts need automated tests?

• Which parts need unit tests?

• Which parts need integration tests?

• Do we need end-to-end tests?

• What linting rules should be enforced?

• What conditions must be met before a pull request can be merged?

4. Performance-related decisions

Examples include:

• Do we need code splitting?

• Do we need virtualization?

• Do we need memoization?

• Is the problem caused by rendering or networking?

• Do we need image optimization?

• Do we need a CDN?

• What caching strategy should be used?

5. Delivery-related decisions

Examples include:

• Should we build this component from scratch?

• Should we use an existing library?

• Should we implement the full solution or a simplified version?

• Is this the right time to refactor?

• Should some improvements be postponed until after the MVP launch?

Every one of these decisions has benefits, costs, and risks.

The main factors behind a good technical decision

1. Project size

A solution that works for a small website may not be suitable for a large platform.

Imagine a simple landing page with five pages.

Does it really need:

• Redux?

• Micro-frontends?

• An event bus?

• A repository pattern?

• A domain layer?

• Complex dependency injection?

• A complete design system?

Probably not.

Now imagine a large platform with:

• Dozens of pages.

• Multiple roles and permissions.

• Several user types.

• Many business features.

• A large engineering team.

• External integrations.

• Real-time updates.

• Payments.

• Complex reporting.

That platform may require a higher level of structure, consistency, and architectural discipline.

The mistake is building a small application as if it were a global enterprise platform, or building a large platform using the same structure as a weekend side project.

2. Team size

Technology selection depends not only on the system, but also on the people who will build and maintain it.

If the team consists of two developers, adding a highly sophisticated architecture may reduce productivity rather than improve it.

If the team consists of thirty developers, the absence of clear boundaries and conventions may lead to:

• Duplicated code.

• Inconsistent implementation styles.

• Conflicting responsibilities.

• Difficult code reviews.

• More defects.

• Slower feature delivery.

In a small team, many issues can be solved through direct communication.

In a large team, technical decisions must be documented and consistently applied.

As the team grows, the following become increasingly important:

• Naming conventions.

• Project structure.

• Shared components.

• Code review guidelines.

• Documentation.

• Testing strategy.

• Ownership.

• Continuous integration checks.

3. Team experience

A technology may be excellent in theory but unsuitable for a team that lacks the experience required to use it effectively.

For example, advanced functional programming may work well in a team that understands:

• Pure functions.

• Immutability.

• Function composition.

• Higher-order functions.

• Algebraic data types.

However, if most of the team is unfamiliar with these concepts, applying them aggressively may produce code that is difficult to understand and maintain.

The same principle applies to:

• RxJS.

• GraphQL.

• Micro-frontends.

• Monorepos.

• WebAssembly.

• Advanced TypeScript types.

• Event-driven architecture.

A successful technical solution must not only be understandable by the person who introduced it.

It must also be understandable, usable, and maintainable by the wider team.

It is easy to write code that makes you look intelligent.

It is much harder to write code that makes the whole team more productive.

4. Maintainability

Code is rarely written once and left untouched.

It is usually:

• Modified.

• Fixed.

• Extended.

• Reused.

• Moved.

• Read by new developers.

• Debugged under production pressure.

Therefore, it is not enough to ask:

Does this solution work?

We should also ask:

• Will it still be understandable in six months?

• Can it be modified without breaking unrelated features?

• Are its responsibilities clear?

• Are there tests protecting critical behavior?

• Does it depend on a stable library?

• Does more than one person understand it?

• Will adding the next feature become easier or harder?

Sometimes the fastest solution to write becomes the most expensive solution to maintain.

At the same time, the most structured solution may be unjustified when the feature is simple, temporary, or unlikely to change.

The goal is not maximum abstraction.

The goal is an appropriate balance of clarity, flexibility, and simplicity.

5. Performance

Performance decisions should not be based on assumptions.

Common statements include:

This approach is slower.

We should use useMemo.

We need to prevent re-renders.

Everything should be lazy loaded.

This library is too large.

Some of these statements may be correct.

Others may have no meaningful effect on the user experience.

Performance should be measured.

Before implementing a performance optimization, ask:

• Where exactly is the problem?

• Is the bottleneck the network?

• Is the JavaScript bundle too large?

• Is rendering expensive?

• Are images poorly optimized?

• Is the API slow?

• Are there too many DOM nodes?

• Does the problem only appear on low-end devices?

• Which metric are we trying to improve?

• What is the value before and after the optimization?

A senior developer does not optimize code simply because it looks theoretically imperfect.

A senior developer optimizes when there is evidence of a real problem or a clearly predictable risk.

6. Delivery time

In real projects, time is a critical factor.

You may have a perfect solution that needs three weeks and a good-enough solution that needs three days.

If the business needs to launch an early version to validate the market, the thre
